verse_text: If a man divorces his wife, and she goes from him to become another man's, may she turn back and return to him again? Would not such a woman be greatly defiled? But you committed fornication with many shepherds and have returned to Me,' says the Lord.

links.context: Fornication, committed by the kings of the earth with this harlot, is a metaphor for infidelity to the Lord ([[JER.3:1]]-5; Ezk 23; Hos 2). The vassal kingdoms within the Roman Empire have accepted the cults of Rome and her emperors and have solidified their obeisance with political and economic obligations--and have fallen into moral decadence. The wine of this fornication has made the inhabitants of the earth to become drunk, seduced and stupefied by a hideous eucharist of death, focused on the persecution and martyrdom of Christians (v. 6; see [[JER.28:7]]).

To "play the harlot" or take a concubine was an expression typically used to paint a prophetic picture of people following false gods, often Baal, whose followers often became involved in extreme sexual misconduct ([[HOS.4:1]]-6; [[JER.3:1]], [[JER.3:8]]; [[EZK.16:41]]; 23:44).
